So basically the adjective and then the thing that is described, right? Mawsoof is the thing that's described and Sifah is the adjective. Meaning, adjective is obviously an English grammatical term, but an adjective describes a noun, right? So you have a noun and then you have the adjective, a word that describes that noun, right?

title: Mawsoof and Sifah: Matching Adjectives and Nouns in Arabic
summary: This session explains how nouns (mawsoof) and adjectives (sifah) must agree in gender, number, case ending, and definiteness. The teacher uses examples from the Quran and the class to illustrate these grammatical rules.
key_points: The mawsoof (described noun) and sifah (adjective) must correspond in four aspects: gender, number, case ending, and definiteness.
Gender agreement: masculine/singular/dual/feminine endings on both the noun and adjective. Example: تاجر مَرْحِم (masculine singular), تاجرت مَرْحِمٍ (feminine singular).
Number agreement: singular, dual, or plural forms must match. Example: تاجر غني (singular), تأجرينا غنيانِ (dual), تجَّارٌ أغنياء (plural).
Case ending agreement: both noun and adjective share the same final short vowel (fatha, kasra, damma). Example: رأيتُ تاجرًا غنيًا (both with fathatan).
Definiteness: if one is definite (e.g., with alif-lam), the other must also be. Example: المَالِكُ الْقُدُّس (definite nouns linked by their attributes).
questions: [00:00:45] Q: What are mawsoof and sifah? — A: Mawsoof is the noun being described, and sifah is the adjective describing it.

quiz.q: What is the definition of مَوْصُوف (mawṣūf) and صِفَة (ṣifah) as explained in the lecture?
options: مَوْصُوف is the noun being described and صِفَة is the adjective describing it
صِفَة is the subject and مَوْصُوف is the object
Both are particles used in sentences
quiz.a: The lecture defined مَوْصُوف as the described noun and صِفَة as the adjective that describes it.

quiz.q: How many aspects must the مَوْصُوف (mawṣūf) and صِفَة (ṣifah) agree in?
quiz.a: The teacher emphasized four: gender, number, case ending (إِعْرَاب), and definiteness.

quiz.q: Which of the following is an example of correct gender agreement?
quiz.a: The lecture explained that if the noun is feminine (تَاجِرَةٌ), the adjective must also be feminine (غَنِيَّةٌ).

quiz.q: What must happen to the صِفَة (ṣifah) when the مَوْصُوف (mawṣūf) becomes dual (مُثَنًّى)?
quiz.a: The lecture showed that in dual form like تَاجِرَانِ, the adjective must also be dual such as غَنِيَانِ.

quiz.q: What does agreement in إِعْرَاب (case ending) mean?
options: Both words must have the same root letters
Both must share the same final vowel (ḥarakah)
quiz.a: The teacher explained that if the noun has fatḥah, kasrah, or ḍammah, the adjective must match it.

quiz.q: In the phrase رَأَيْتُ تَاجِرًا غَنِيًّا, why does غَنِيًّا have fatḥah?
options: Because it matches the case of تَاجِرًا
quiz.a: The lecture showed that the adjective follows the noun in case ending, so both take fatḥah.

quiz.q: What happens if the مَوْصُوف (mawṣūf) is definite (مَعْرِفَة)?
options: The صِفَة must also be definite
quiz.a: The lecture emphasized that both must match in definiteness, e.g., التَّاجِرُ الْغَنِيُّ.

quiz.q: Why is التَّاجِرُ غَنِيٌّ not considered مَوْصُوف وَصِفَة?
options: Because it is a verbal sentence
Because it forms a مُبْتَدَأ وَخَبَر (subject–predicate) sentence
quiz.a: The lecture explained this is a complete sentence meaning “the merchant is rich,” not a noun-adjective phrase.

quiz.q: What is the key difference in word order between English and Arabic adjectives?
options: Arabic places the adjective before the noun
Arabic places the adjective after the noun
Both languages use the same order
quiz.a: The teacher explained that Arabic says the noun first (مَوْصُوف) then the adjective (صِفَة), unlike English.

quran_examples.translation: And He will bring a new creation.
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates agreement between خَلْقٍ (khalq, mawṣūf) and جَدِيدٍ (jadīd, ṣifah) in gender, number, case (kasrah), and indefiniteness.

quran_examples.translation: When it sees them from a distant place.
quran_examples.note: Illustrates مَكَانٍ (makān, mawṣūf) and بَعِيدٍ (baʿīd, ṣifah) matching in gender, number, case, and indefiniteness.

quran_examples.arabic: هُوَ اللَّهُ الَّذِي لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ الْمَلِكُ الْقُدُّوسُ
quran_examples.transliteration: huwa Allāhu alladhī lā ilāha illā huwa al-malik al-quddūs
quran_examples.translation: He is Allāh عَزَّ وَجَلَّ, other than whom there is no deity, the Sovereign, the Most Pure.
quran_examples.note: Shows definite agreement where الْمَلِكُ (al-malik) and الْقُدُّوسُ (al-quddūs) are both definite and match as noun and adjective.
Demonstrates agreement in feminine gender, singular form, case (kasrah), and indefiniteness between فَاكِهَةٍ and كَثِيرَةٍ.
